questions.text: I hold the view that being strategically selfish is a prerequisite for long-term effectiveness.
I am deeply moved by the sight of someone responding to another's distress.
I find that prioritizing my own comfort over others' needs feels wrong.
I tend to figure things out as I go rather than reading instructions first.
I build review cycles into my work to catch errors before they compound.
I hold the view that emotional detachment during crises is a form of professionalism that is particularly valuable in contexts where multiple lives or livelihoods are at stake.
I usually enter debates by asking questions rather than stating positions.
I hold the view that a clear plan reduces wasted effort and increases impact.
I hold the view that my emotional range, while perhaps narrower, is no less genuine than anyone else's.
I believe that the moral concern generated by witnessing hardship is a resource that should be channeled, not suppressed.
I can tell when a person adjusts their level of formality mid-conversation in response to a social cue they received.
I hold the view that treating suffering as a genuine moral priority — in every decision, every day — is the standard to which I hold myself.
I feel that building a personal reputation for excellence is one of my top priorities.
I feel that the group should find its own way rather than rely on one person.
I feel that a democratic team outperforms a top-down one.
I find that my emotional responses to hypothetical scenarios are nearly as strong as my responses to real events.
I routinely track my personal progress and set benchmarks for my own improvement.
I often skip meals or sleep to help someone who is counting on me.
I tend to focus on the information in an email rather than the tone.
I enjoy naming the problem and telling the group how we will solve it.
I detect when someone's laughter is slightly delayed, as if they are calculating whether to join in.
I sense when someone is withholding information based on who else is present.
I execute new procedures and learn from the actual experience of doing them.
I feel most useful when I am calling the shots under pressure.
I find that even in my most emotionally intense moments, there is a layer of me that stays observational and calm.
I do not naturally track whose opinion shifted during a discussion and why.
I routinely pick up litter, fix broken things, and tidy shared spaces without being asked.
I find that I am more interested in the outcome of a discussion than in how people felt during it.
I feel that my willingness to take command in uncertain moments is what makes me effective.
I hold the view that objective evaluation of distressing circumstances is a form of intellectual honesty that serves everyone better than emotional reaction.
I find that unresolved emotional business from the morning can cast a pall over my entire day.
I gauge progress by feel rather than comparing actual results to a predetermined timeline.
I write end-of-day summaries to capture what was accomplished and what's pending.
I carry the emotional tone of a dream well into my waking day.
I find that I naturally default to assessing what can be done rather than dwelling on how a situation feels.
I find that the emotional charge of a memory fades quickly, leaving only the facts.
